Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 67 of about 123 target actions so far.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 67 actions returned the board to a state already seen 67 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 67 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

The game says "The game is over" but the player is still on level 2. Let me check the last action result more carefully. The player moved LEFT from cols 24-28 to cols 19-23. The player is now at rows 5-6, cols 19-23.
Wait, looking at the image, the player (orange+blue) appears to have moved to a new position - it's now in the lower-middle area at rows 35-36, cols 29-33 approximately. Let me check the actual state.
Actually looking more carefully at the image, the orange+blue player is now at the bottom-center area. The game says "game is over" - let me check if this means level completed or something else.
Let me inspect the current state more carefully.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what happened - game says "game over"
print(f"Last action result: {last_action_result}")

# Check player position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O' and n['pixels'] > 5:
        b = n['boundary']
        min_row = min(p[0] for p in b)
        max_row = max(p[0] for p in b)
        min_col = min(p[1] for p in b)
        max_col = max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"Player at rows {min_row}-{max_row}, cols {min_col}-{max_col}")

# Check all colored objects
print("\nAll notable objects:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] not in ['B', 'G', 'w']:
        b = n['boundary']
        min_row = min(p[0] for p in b)
        max_row = max(p[0] for p in b)
        min_col = min(p[1] for p in b)
        max_col = max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min_row}-{max_row}, cols {min_col}-{max_col}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

The game is over (GAME_OVER state). The player moved to rows 40-41, cols 29-33. But the game says GAME_OVER with done=False and level_completed=False. This seems like the game ended prematurely or there's some issue.
Looking at the state:
- Player (O) at rows 40-41, cols 29-33
- Blue (b) at rows 42-44, cols 29-33 (15 pixels) - this is below the player
- Blue (b) at rows 41-43, cols 15-17 (5 pixels) - this is the blue 'L' shape near the yellow square
- Yellow (Y) at rows 16-18, cols 15-17 (8 pixels) - top-left yellow square
- Yellow (Y) at rows 51-53, cols 40-42 (8 pixels) - bottom yellow square
- White (W) at rows 46-47, cols 51-52 (3 pixels) - the white plus sign
The game is over. The state says 'GAME_OVER'. But level_completed is False. This is confusing. Let me check if there's a way to continue or if I need to restart.
Wait - looking at the executed_actions, it shows 54 cells changed with 1 frame. That's a lot of cells changed for a simple LEFT move. And the player position jumped from rows 5-6 to rows 40-41. This suggests the player fell down or something happened.
The game is over. Let me check if there are any more levels or if this is the end. Since game_over=True but level_completed=False and done=False, maybe the game just ended (like the player died or ran out of moves).
Looking at the timer bar at rows 61-62, it seems like there's a countdown. The yellow bar is at rows 61-62, cols 13-54 (84 pixels). Maybe the timer ran out?
Actually, the game says "game_over" which could mean the player lost. Let me check the history to see what happened.
Actually wait - looking more carefully, the player was at rows 5-6, cols 19-23 before the last LEFT action. After LEFT, the player is at rows 40-41, cols 29-33. That's a huge jump - the player teleported somewhere. And the game is now over.
This might be a "death" state - the player fell into a pit or something. The game is over and I can't continue. Let me check if there's a way to restart or if the level needs to be replayed.
Since the game is over, I should probably stop and report the status. But let me first check if there are any valid actions that could restart the game.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game is over - check if we can continue
print(f"Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over')}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done')}")
print(f"Valid actions: {valid_actions}")

# The game ended. Let me check the transition to understand what happened
if last_transition:
    print(f"\nLast transition action: {last_transition.action}")
    before_seg = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
    after_seg = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation
    
    # Find player before
    for n in before_seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'O' and n['pixels'] > 5:
            b = n['boundary']
            print(f"Player BEFORE: rows {min(p[0] for p in b)}-{max(p[0] for p in b)}, cols {min(p[1] for p in b)}-{max(p[1] for p in b)}")
    
    # Find player after
    for n in after_seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'O' and n['pixels'] > 5:
            b = n['boundary']
            print(f"Player AFTER: rows {min(p[0] for p in b)}-{max(p[0] for p in b)}, cols {min(p[1] for p in b)}-{max(p[1] for p in b)}")

# Since game is over, let's try to see if any action can restart
print(f"\nTrying to see if game can continue...")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
